attributes.stem:
A 38-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner with gradually worsening dysphagia, and frequent regurgitation of food while eating and at night. Over the last few months he has dropped two pant sizes even though he has not been trying to lose weight. He otherwise has an insignificant medical history.

He is referred to a specialist and undergoes an endoscopy that subsequently rules out malignancy. He is then sent for oesophageal manometry which indicates that he has excessive lower esophageal sphincter tone and intermittent periods of panoesophageal pressurisation.

When asked, he explains that he wants one procedure and does not want repeat interventions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has achalasia. There are a number of factors to consider which will help decide the most appropriate plan of management. These factors include the age and general state of health of the patient and the manometric findings.

Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM) - Incorrect. This a first-line option for the surgical management of achalasia. However, it has a higher likelihood of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease post-procedure and for this reason would not be used in this case. There is a 40-50% lifetime risk of reflux requiring PPI therapy. POEM can also be performed for symptom relief for patients who have previously had a laparoscopic Heller myotomy.

Pneumatic dilatation of the oesophagus - Incorrect. This is a management option recommended for people with type II achalasia. However, it has a more favourable response in people over the age of 45 and those of female sex. There is a high likelihood that this patient would need further interventions as remission only last 5-10 years following pneumatic dilatation, and this is contraindicated by his desire for a single procedure.

Botox injection - Incorrect. This is a non-surgical management of achalasia. While it is a possible management option for type II achalasia, it is generally recommended for patients not fit for surgery or as as bridge to more definitive therapy (e.g. in a pregnant patient). It is a treatment that requires repeat doses and therefore goes against his wishes for a single procedure.

Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy - Correct. This is an option for the surgical management of achalasia. This patient has Type II achalasia and would be classified as level III as per the Eckardt score. The recommended management of type II achalasia is pneumatic dilatation, Heller myotomy or POEM. As he is young and otherwise healthy he is a good candidate for Heller myotomy.

Nissen fundoplication - Incorrect. This is the surgical management offered to patients with gastro-oesophageal reflux. This patient’s of regurgitation indication material coming up from the oesophagus and not the stomach.
learningPoints: Achalasia type II can be managed surgically with a Heller Myotomy.
Laparoscopic Heller myotomy is preferred for patients desiring a single, definitive procedure for achalasia.
references: Zaninotto G, Bennett C, Boeckxstaens G, Costantini M, Ferguson MK, Pandolfino JE, Patti MG, Ribeiro U Jr, Richter J, Swanstrom L, Tack J, Triadafilopoulos G, Markar SR, Salvador R, Faccio L, Andreollo NA, Cecconello I, Costamagna G, da Rocha JRM, Hungness ES, Fisichella PM, Fuchs KH, Gockel I, Gurski R, Gyawali CP, Herbella FAM, Holloway RH, Hongo M, Jobe BA, Kahrilas PJ, Katzka DA, Dua KS, Liu D, Moonen A, Nasi A, Pasricha PJ, Penagini R, Perretta S, Sallum RAA, Sarnelli G, Savarino E, Schlottmann F, Sifrim D, Soper N, Tatum RP, Vaezi MF, van Herwaarden-Lindeboom M, Vanuytsel T, Vela MF, Watson DI, Zerbib F, Gittens S, Pontillo C, Vermigli S, Inama D, Low DE. The 2018 ISDE achalasia guidelines. Dis Esophagus. 2018 Sep 1;31(9). doi: 10.1093/dote/doy071. PMID: 30169645. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30169645/

Patel DA, Lappas BM, Vaezi MF. An Overview of Achalasia and Its Subtypes. Gastroenterol Hepatol (N Y). 2017 Jul;13(7):411-421. PMID: 28867969; PMCID: PMC5572971. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28867969/

Rebecchi F, Allaix ME, Schlottmann F, Patti MG, Morino M. Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy and Fundoplication: What Is the Evidence? Am Surg. 2018 Apr 1;84(4):481-488. PMID: 29712593. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29712593/

Valverde A, Cahais J, Lupinacci R, Goasguen N, Oberlin O. Laparoscopic Heller myotomy. J Visc Surg. 2018 Feb;155(1):59-64. doi: 10.1016/j.jviscsurg.2018.01.006. Epub 2018 Feb 21. PMID: 29475748. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29475748/
